author: @hellobeen
text:
I've built myself a very large printer a few years ago.. First thing i see:
is there a built plate you can take of? Use a printing surface? Something like a sheet of paper or plastic and clip it with decent clips onto the build plate.. 
if a print gets really stuck on the bed, you almost have to destroy the entire printer to get it of the (heated?) bed...
I had a very thin piece of plywood with blue painters tape on it, so you can take the entire print out, and as the panel bends, it's easy to release the print from the plate..

author: @gertvantonder562
text: I'm from South Africa and working for DKT Engineering. We bought a Mingda MD1000 printer. After waiting for about 2 months it finally arrived. I was very excited to unpack and power it up. It's a monster with a build volume of 1mx1mx1m. Unfortunately after I've been using it for about a week I found out that printing anything round comes out oval. At first I thought setting the steps of the X and Y steppers will solve the problem but that was where the simplesty of solving the issue ended 😂 Square objects measure size in the X axis and Y axis so changing the steps would not solve the problem. I got the experts in and they started by setting the tension on the drive belts and doing other stuff for about 2.5 days and they left saying that they could not find any solution for the problem. So after paying 700k in South African rand (ZAR) for the machine it's just standing in a room next to my office for the last two weeks. I'm planning on building a Voron 2.5 and after completion I'll show them a printer can do. My personal opinion is that big machines aren't as accurate as smaller ones. Hopefully you will have better luck with your machine Robert. Love watching your videos my friend. Keep them coming

author: @WhyteHorse2023
text: I worry about temperature fluctuations next to the window because it's a large metal contraption with a thermal coefficient of expansion. People with large CNC machines put them in temperature controlled rooms. Anyway, these Chines made products designed for export to the US have to meet certain electrical standards, hence the breaker switch and heavy gauge wires.

author: @jwc4520
text: For a mad scientist,  I must say your home looks quite in order. My first experience with 3 D printing was about 32 years  ago, set up a convention for 3D printing . I wS amazed at what they could do one made ball bearing races out of a ceramic material, printing a wax in the voids , tap it on the table the wax fell out turned to dust at room temperature.  All the parts for a small block Chevy engine , now that was tempting  1/10 scale . As a modeler I could see a tiny dragster ...take care . In my years of setting up such things I learned to wire in many nationalities ...what's life without a challenge?

author: @mikeselectricstuff
text: That's a 16A C19 plug - not proprietory. I wonder if they used that as the heated bed pulls more than the 10A rating of a normal IEC - the European Schuko plug that was fitted is rated for 16A - if it's over 10A it could be marginal for  a 13A socket .

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @CrudelyMade
text:
this is more a thought for elegoo than you.. but since a lot of heat is lost from the bed to the atmophere.. and I wonder if instead of enclosing the whole thing (Still a large volume to heat) if it could have a 'top' that rises with the extruder, and maybe accordion sides that rise with the z axis.  basically creating the smallest volume to heat and retain heat while adding neat zero mass for the printer to work with. 
would make bed heating faster and might work better with some materials like petg or abs. ;-) 
if you have contacts, please pass it on!

author: @quantumenergysolutions9128
text: Phew! At least you only need to do that once! Please can you attempt a Kinetic Buoyancy Gen ? Ive gone over the Maths with GPT and can show you proof, this company have certificates and are selling this device in Germany. (see)" Free Energy Generator - Buoyancy / Water Displacement Power Plant producing 5kW of electric energy".  The size of machine is determined by  LPM of compressed air you have, just need to test how much water it can displace in 1-2 seconds. That determines the size of the containers . Then if its going to be eg 3m high, and you have eg. 10L containers,  and  20 total containers(10 going up & 10 going down) , then the side filled with air will have 10L x 10 = 100KG of buoyancy force at all times . The higher the machine is the more efficient it gets. it achieves 10x OU +.  People say cant get more out than in, thats true , it fills up the buoyant side bit by bit and stores a hell of a lot of force that it dumps at the top , one by one!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i will have a think about it mate - lots of projects to do!
